elements.sent-2411-002: In this paper, different variants of reversible finite automata are compared, and their hierarchy by the expressive power is established.
elements.sent-2411-003: It is shown that one-way reversible automata with multiple initial states (MRFA) recognize strictly more languages than sweeping reversible automata (sRFA), which are in turn stronger than one-way reversible automata with a single initial state (1RFA).
elements.sent-2411-004: The latter recognize strictly more languages than one-way permutation automata (1PerFA).
elements.sent-2411-005: It is also shown that the hierarchy of sRFA by the number of passes over the input string collapses: it turns out that three passes are always enough.
elements.sent-2411-006: On the other hand, MRFA form a hierarchy by the number of initial states: their subclass with at most $k$ initial states (MRFA$^k$) recognize strictly fewer languages than MRFA$^{k + 1}$, and also MRFA$^k$ are incomparable with sRFA.
elements.sent-2411-007: In the unary case, sRFA, MRFA$^k$ and MRFA become equal in their expressive power, and the inclusion of 1RFA into sRFA remains proper.
elements.sent-2411-008: Classical one-way deterministic automata (1DFA) have an interesting variant: reversible automata with one or multiple initial states (1RFA, MRFA), which were first studied by Angluin~\cite{Angluin and by Pin~\cite{Pin1987 .}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Such automata model reversible computation, which is important to study, because reversibility is the only potential way to create computer hardware which does not heat up during work.
elements.sent-2411-010: Indeed, according to Landauer's principle~\cite{Landauer irreversible erasure of one bit of information causes generation of a certain amount of heat, and this physical limit can be overcome only by logically reversible devices.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Among recent studies of 1RFA, there is a paper by H\'eam~\cite{Heam on the relative succinctness of 1DFA and 1RFA.}

elements.sent-2411-015: Another model of reversible automata studied in the literature are the one-way permutation automata (1PerFA), defined by an additional restriction: unlike reversible automata, 1PerFA always read input data until the end, that is, their transition function is fully defined and hence forms a permutation on the set of states.
elements.sent-2411-016: One-way permutation automata were first studied by Thierrin~\cite{Thierrin , and a family of languages recognized by 1PerFA is called the group languages, because the syntactic monoid of such automata is a group.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Recently, Jecker, Mazzocchi and Wolf~\cite{JeckerMazzocchiWolf investigated the computational complexity of the decomposition problem for permutation automata.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Also, Hospod\'ar and Mlyn\'ar\v{c ik~\cite{HospodarMlynarcik studied the state complexity of operations on permutation automata, while Rauch and Holzer~\cite{RauchHolzer obtained the complexity of operations on permutation automata in the number of accepting states.}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Models of automata can be compared in their expressive power, that is, by inclusion of the families of languages recognized by different models.
elements.sent-2411-020: One-way reversible automata recognize strictly fewer languages than classical deterministic automata: for example, they cannot recognize a regular language $a^*b^*$.
elements.sent-2411-021: At the same time, permutation automata turn out to be an even more constrained model: in particular, they cannot recognize any finite language, whereas reversible automata recognize all finite languages.
elements.sent-2411-022: In this paper, this hierarchy is completed by other variants of deterministic, reversible and permutation automata.
elements.sent-2411-023: Among the models considered, there are also two-way reversible and permutation automata.
elements.sent-2411-024: Classical two-way deterministic automata (2DFA) can return to previously read symbols of an input string while processing it.
elements.sent-2411-025: The possibility to read a string in both directions does not allow 2DFA to recognize more languages than one-way deterministic automata.
elements.sent-2411-026: But as soon as reversible automata are allowed to read a string in both directions, they can recognize all regular languages, as was shown by Kondacs and Watrous~\cite{KondacsWatrous , which exceeds the expressive power of one-way reversible automata (1RFA).}
elements.sent-2411-027: Between one-way and two-way automata, an intermediate model of sweeping automata~\cite{Sipser was studied.}
elements.sent-2411-028: These automata alternate between reading the input string from left to right and from right to left.
elements.sent-2411-029: Recently, a sweeping variant of permutation automata (2PerFA) was studied by the authors~\cite{RadionovaOkhotin , and it was shown that the new model recognizes exactly the same languages as 1PerFA.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Another variant of automata is that with multiple initial states: these are one-way automata that can choose non-deterministically one of their initial states before reading an input string.
elements.sent-2411-031: Deterministic automata with multiple initial states (MDFA)~\cite{HolzerSalomaaYu recognize exactly the family of regular languages, that is, their expressive power is the same as 1DFA's.}

elements.section-2411-005: Hierarchy of MRFA with a bounded number of initial states
elements.sent-2411-277: In this section, a hierarchy of MRFA in the number of initial states is obtained.
elements.sent-2411-278: The bottom level of this hierarchy is 1RFA, that is, MRFA with one initial state.
elements.sent-2411-279: The $k$-th level contains MRFA with at most $k$ initial states (MRFA$^k$).
elements.sent-2411-280: The entire hierarchy is contained in the class of MRFA with unbounded number of initial states.
elements.sent-2411-281: And, as shown by the following example, every additional initial state increases the expressive power of the model.
elements.sent-2411-282: For each $k \geqslant 2$, the language $L_k = \bigcup_{i = 1 ^k (ab^i)^*$ is recognized by an MRFA with $k$ initial states, but no MRFA with fewer than $k$ initial states recognizes this language.}
elements.sent-2411-283: An MRFA$^k$ recognizing the language $L_k = \bigcup_{i = 1 ^k (ab^i)^*$ accepts the strings from each set $(ab^i)^*$ from a separate initial state.}
elements.sent-2411-284: For this, in each $i$-th initial state, there is a cycle by a single string $ab^i$.
elements.sent-2411-285: Suppose, for the sake of a contradiction, that there exists an MRFA $\mathcal{A}$ with at most $k - 1$ initial states, and $\mathcal{A}$ recognizes $L_k$.
elements.sent-2411-286: For each $i$, with $1 \leqslant i \leqslant k$, fix any initial state of the automaton $\mathcal{A}$ from which it accepts infinitely many strings from a set $(ab^i)^* \subset L_k$.
elements.sent-2411-288: Then, by reading a string long enough from $(ab^i)^*$, the automaton loops in $q_i$ by a string $ab^i$.
elements.sent-2411-289: Thus, for each $i$, there is a cycle in the initial state $q_i$ by some power of a string $ab^i$, and the state $q_i$ itself must be accepting.
elements.sent-2411-290: There are in total $k$ different indices, but $\mathcal{A}$ has at most $k - 1$ initial states.
elements.sent-2411-291: Hence, there is some initial state $q_i$ with cycles by some power of the string $ab^i$ and by another power of the string $ab^{i'}$, with $i \neq i'$.
elements.sent-2411-292: Accordingly, we obtain that from this initial state the automaton $\mathcal{A}$ accepts a string from the language $(ab^i)^+ (ab^{i' )^+$, and such a string not in $L_k$, a contradiction.}
elements.sent-2411-293: Therefore, there is indeed no MRFA with at most $k - 1$ initial states for the language $L_k$.
elements.sent-2411-294: Earlier, it has been proved that the class of sRFA is included in MRFA.
elements.sent-2411-295: Now, the intermediate classes of MRFA$^k$ have been added to the hierarchy, and a question arises, how do they compare to sRFA.
elements.sent-2411-296: It is already known from Example~\ref{example:a*-cup-b* that MRFA$^2$ are not contained in sRFA.}
elements.sent-2411-297: And could sRFA be simulated by MRFA with at most $k$ initial states? The answer is given in the next example.
elements.sent-2411-298: For each $k \geqslant 2$, the language $L_k = \bigcup_{i = 0 ^{k - 1 (ab^i)^* ba^i$ is recognized by sRFA, but it is not recognized by any MRFA with fewer than $k$ initial states.}}
